lesson.learn: Some animals have an incredible ability to survive being frozen solid, a phenomenon known as "antifreeze" proteins. This remarkable adaptation helps them withstand harsh winter conditions and even allows some species to be frozen for extended periods without suffering any harm. The importance of this trait lies in its unique survival mechanism, which is different from hibernation or other forms of dormancy. Scientists have been studying these animals to understand the underlying biology behind their ability.
lesson.simpler: Imagine being able to turn into a popsicle and then coming back to life when it thaws! That's basically what some animals can do, but they don't freeze like ice cream; instead, special proteins in their bodies protect them from getting damaged by the cold. These proteins are like built-in antifreeze machines that help these creatures survive in freezing temperatures.
lesson.keyTakeaway: The key to an animal's ability to survive being frozen solid lies in its production of specific "antifreeze" proteins, which prevent ice crystals from forming within their cells and tissues.
lesson.deeperDive: Research has shown that certain species, such as wood frogs (Rana sylvatica), have developed unique antifreeze proteins in response to their cold environments. These proteins work by binding to water molecules and preventing them from freezing into ice crystals, which would cause cell damage. In some cases, animals can even reabsorb lost body fluids when they thaw out, allowing them to recover quickly. Wood frogs, for example, can freeze solid during winter and then thaw in the spring to resume their normal activities. Scientists have also discovered that certain fish species, like Antarctic icefish (Channichthyidae), produce antifreeze proteins that are similar to those found in wood frogs.
lesson.funFact: Believe it or not, some species of frogs can even freeze their bodies and then thaw out when spring arrives, allowing them to restart their metabolic processes!

people: 1. Dr. John Storey (Professor of Biochemistry at the University of Washington)
2. Dr. David D. Cai (Research Scientist at the University of Toronto)
3. Dr. Lisa M. Schulte (Professor of Biology at Iowa State University)
places: 1. Woodland habitats in North America, where wood frogs are commonly found
2. The Antarctic continent, home to icefish species like Channichthyidae
3. The Arctic Circle, where some animals have adapted to survive extreme cold
years: 1. 1960s - When scientists first discovered antifreeze proteins in fish
2. 1990s - Researchers identified similar proteins in wood frogs
3. 2015 - A team of scientists published a study on the genetic basis of antifreeze protein production in icefish
